note:
星期六早上19月19日在惠灵顿度过了一个很酷的春日。这是赫尔曼Knippenberg退休的第一天，他期待着早餐的煎饼。那是电话响了。这是来自奥克兰的朋友的呼叫，通知他，连环杀手查尔斯·索伯拉·斯托尔·刚刚在尼泊尔被捕。
 “Nunc Aut Nunquam，”赫尔曼在那一天说。 “这是现在或永无止境的情况。”
 赫尔曼经常说这样的事情。在70年代初，高大和可爱的人，他的方式就在粗暴的友好的一面。赫尔曼喜欢良好的短语。他在英语，拉丁语和德语中有一个无休止的库存。
 我在十年前第一次见到赫尔曼，当我在Unity Books Wellington工作时。文学谈话在那里很常见，所以我有一天在我的肩膀上听到荷兰重音的声音并不感到惊讶，询问我们有多少小说帕特里克麦格拉斯。他也是Heinrichböll的粉丝。很快我们正在交换标题并穿过架子。
 赫尔曼有一本书的书籍，他被追求并拔出了一个带有出版商的名字和发布日期的笔记本。如果您熟悉工作零售，您将知道这并不总是一个好兆头。但主题赫尔曼跟踪总是有趣的。他会流行进入，我们聊天聊天Graham Greene或Carlos The Jackal，或者文学犯罪小说撒上间谍活动。
 一旦，我们被一本书通知了一本书赫尔曼已下令的书将要迟到几天，我得到了我的第一个线索，即赫曼可以罕见。一个不容易接受的男人。 “这不是关于他们在告诉你的原因，”赫尔曼说，相信，必须有某种方式立即获得这本书。 “这是关于你的想法！”
 我们成为了朋友。我会去下午的下午茶。外面有一个白色的纠察围栏，窗户舒适的椅子，始终是各种各样的饼干。我了解到赫尔曼一直是荷兰政府的职业外交官。他住在雅典，卢森堡，维也纳，纽约，雅加达和泰国。 1987年，他遇到了他的第二任妻子Vanessa  - 一个猕猴桃 - 在维也纳。而且也是职业外交官，就像动画为赫尔曼一样，她当时发布到新西兰大使馆。 
关于赫尔曼的事情并不是每天都有一些事情。有时他会说“像你所知道的那样，当时我在曼谷。”或者，“因为我的参与案件。”赫尔曼本人从未在他的生命故事的深处放弃了我。我很欣赏。这是一个在书店的同事，他提到了Herman有自己的一篇维基百科页面。它只是慢慢地，一个线索导致另一个人，我了解到赫曼曾经有助于抓住连环杀手。
 “好消息是你的智力水平很高，”赫尔曼在心理评价中被告知。 “坏消息可能是你遇到了你无法尊重的人的权威问题。”
 在20世纪70年代，查尔斯Sobhraj是亚洲最受欢迎的男性之一。多个国家的警察曾试过，未能逮捕这种复杂的康曼，因为他对嬉皮士踪迹的年轻旅行者的潜伏期。在媒体中，他被各种众不同地称为比基尼杀手，分离器和蛇，绰号受到他的可怕方法和湿滑的脱发。在他捕获的时候，Sobhraj有各种各样的中毒，淹死，勒死，刺伤12人。至少12人。
 多年来，有两部电影，四部纪录片和几本关于Sobhraj的书 - 一个常年迷人的怪物，他几乎似乎定制了犯罪惊悚片。 Netflix现在正在筛查蛇，这是一个八集团的BBC共同生产，这是第一次突出赫尔曼在逮捕Sobhraj中的关键作用。
 关于串行杀手的故事是一个Dime-of。关于抓住它们的真正人的故事很少。当我让赫尔曼告诉我他在案件中的角色时，他在特征上准备了。
 赫尔曼可以详细阐明索波拉姆的每篇文章，并具有一个年轻的律师或调查记者的热情。 “我们将制作一个能够承受审查的作品，”他宣称。他经常回溯并符合他对事实精度的陈述。
 在一系列的电话上，他越来越多地投资这篇文章 - 不仅仅是其准确性，而且在他的故事中被告知。他有一个标题的建议，以及故事如何开始的想法。他坚持认为我们认识到泰国的加拿大大使，几乎没有提到书籍或文章。 （“比尔鲍尔。不要忘记他有多重要。他是强大的。西方的一座柱子。”） 
在我们的第一次谈话开始时，我可以听到在背景中重新排列的文件。
 “我已经准备好了。射击。我手头有一切。就个人而言，我认为我们应该从索波拉姆回到我的生活中。他在加德满都被捕的那一天。那是新西兰警方所涉及的时候。我记得清楚。“
  “是的。这是2003年9月19日星期六的早晨。惠灵顿的一个酷春日。这是我退休的第一天，我期待着一顿早餐的煎饼。那是电话响了。“
  Herman Knippenberg出生于荷兰的一个小镇的Bresukhuizen，靠近德国边境。他的父亲是医生和荷兰抵抗的成员。老虎坦克将滚过镇，SS官员会敲门，以问他父亲的下落。 1944年10月，当赫尔曼六个月大时，他的家庭被摧毁，可能是一枚盟军炸弹在沉船之战中掉下来。他的母亲把他放在婴儿车里，走了50公里的祖父母。这就是赫曼被提出的地方。
 赫尔曼一直毕业于他的班级，但从他最早的几年来，他喜欢自己的方式做事。 “我总是一个困难的孩子，”他说。 “我的母亲会给出一个指导，我会做一个相反的。我不会发出问题 - 我会做得非常不同。在三到五个之间，我会纠正任何人犯错误，把我最喜欢的书读给我。 “不，那不是作者所说的，”我会说。我会让他们知道！“
 19岁，通过叙利亚和土耳其搭乘三个月，在埃及支出两个月后，赫尔曼申请了外交服务。 “在我的心理测试中，他们说，”好消息是你的智力水平很高“，”他回忆道。 “”坏消息可能是您遇到权威的问题，从您无法尊重的人。“
 1976年，在他的职业生涯中仍在32岁，仍然在他的职业生涯中，当被要求询问曼谷的荷兰大使馆曾在曼谷举行的初级外交官，他被要求询问泰国的两名荷兰背包客：Henk Bintanja和他的女朋友Cornelia Hemker。他们的家人担心：六个星期没有任何信徒 - 不是Cornelia的母亲和妹妹的圣诞节或生日。基于自己作为背包客的经验，赫尔曼的直觉告诉他一些事情。他觉得受到亲属和职责的推动。 
这对夫妇最后的联系人提出了一位法国人在香港，他以低价销售宝石，并邀请他们加入泰国。赫尔曼了解到，亨克和克诺塞利亚从未到达酒店，他们在抵达卡上列出。他还知道几个月前，两个游客的尸体，据说澳大利亚人在曼谷发现。当地新闻界有文章，但没有人在内的泰国警察有任何领导。
 “正如他们在荷兰所说，他们听到了铃声的响声，但尚未知道梆子挂在哪里。”
 细节抓住了赫尔曼的注意力：其中一个死的游客一直穿着荷兰制造的T恤。他联系了澳大利亚大使馆，并被告知，最初怀疑受害者的两种澳大利亚人仍然活着。所以赫尔曼获得了失踪的荷兰游客的牙科记录，并用它们来证实他们属于烧焦仍然坐在曼谷太平间。赫尔曼本人去了识别尸体。 “我永远不会忘记它，”他说。
 从理论上讲，这就是赫曼作为外交官员的角色应该结束，因为泰国警方接管了。但赫尔曼怀疑警察被充分激励，所以他留在案件。
 一旦他开始调查此事，他就会听到泰国其他可疑背包客杀手的谣言，好像有一个忌狗跟踪嬉皮士。一个名叫特蕾莎·克顿的年轻女子被吸毒并被绑架，然后穿着比基尼，留在海滩上。 “赫克顿已经成为你所知道的尼姑！”赫尔曼说。当他第一次告诉我这个时，我不确定是否相信他。另一个年轻的旅行者，Vitali Hakim的身体被发现在路边被烧毁。
 荷兰游客被确定后不久，一个男人和女人使用受害者的护照飞往尼泊尔。这对夫妇在五天后回到了泰国，在另外两个背包的护照，美国康妮布朗塞和她的加拿大朋友Laurent Carriere。它会出现，Bronzich和Carriere也被谋杀了：刺伤多次并被责备超越认可。
 赫尔曼的调查在他自己生命中的各个方面都拉动。他的第一个妻子安吉拉是支持的，并帮助研究了这项研究，但赫曼越来越多地走。大使馆的上司越来越沮丧，即他显然更专注于追逐凶手而不是履行他的行政职责。 
在比利时大使馆的一个好朋友，赫尔曼听到了一个名叫艾伦盖拉的可疑角色。他还有一个公寓的话，荷兰夫妇可能会留下来。它作为旅行者各方的频繁网站的声誉，由一位名叫艾伦盖尔的神秘法国人主持。大楼的邻居报道了尖叫声。
 事实证明，公寓的各方主人是一个不同的艾伦盖拉。但是，不可思议的是，联系领导了赫尔曼到了犯罪现场。
 “我无法相信巧合。这是一个evus ex machina。喜欢廉价的飞机小说！“
 赫尔曼被泰国警方获得许可进入空公寓。他组建了一个行动团队 - 他自己，他的比利时朋友，一个美国驻驻邻居之一。里面，他们发现了12个西部护照和大型塑料浴缸的镇静剂和注射器。赫尔曼回忆起在床柱上看到刮痕标记，以至于他后来意识到被戴上手铐到床上的受害者的意识。
 赫尔曼将他的研究发现到了泰国警察，并迫使他们逮捕。但是，当面对时，“Alan Gaultier”以大卫艾伦戈尔的名义制作了一个伪造的美国护照。这种欺骗让他走出泰国。
 当然，他的名字不是Alan Gaultier或David Alan Gore，但Charles Sobhraj。到目前为止，他知道当局是在他的踪迹中 - 并变得更加棱来的暴力。接下来，他在印度突然出现并在一个以色列游客的护照上徘徊，他们在那里失踪了。他还致命地毒害了一个名叫Jean-Luc所罗门的法国人。印度警察被警告。然后，1976年7月，有两个新的同伙，Sohraj将自己暗示为新德里22名学生的法国旅游组的当地指南。他为他们提供了抗痢疾用药，并故意过度过度剂量。随着受害者偶然地偶然地击败了Vikram酒店的大厅，他被三个派对在酒吧举行，直到印度警察到达并逮捕了他。
 赫尔曼刚从加拿大大使，比尔鲍尔拜访的钓鱼之旅。 “印度警察也一直在钓鱼，”鲍尔告诉他。 “而且你不会相信他们抓到了！” 
法国巡回群体幸存下来，但Sobrhaj被谋杀了Jean-Luc所罗门。这就是他的完全身份终于光明的方式。
 要说Sobhraj享受他的臭名昭着是轻描淡写的。他以一种让你的皮肤爬行的方式在它中旋转。
 Charles Sobhraj的印度越南血统出生于西贡。他部分地由法国军队继父提出，并在印度支那和法国作为一个孩子之间。被囚禁为小少年的小犯罪，他贿赂后卫让他的细胞更舒适。在他发布后，他向东南亚带来了一个随行人员，其中包括他的弟弟和各种女朋友和共同之处。他们建立了一种抢劫珠宝店的模式，被抓住，假装疾病和逃脱监狱。
 Sobhraj在曼谷削减了Debonair Figure，在社会和后街中同样舒适。他讲了五种语言。他体育着着色的太阳镜和贝雷帽，高领衫和运动夹克。护照是他的货币，身份的目标。他用摇滚明星招手突然进入旅行者的生活。他能够组织任何东西;宝石，毒品，睡觉的床。他的受害者通常是那些试图抵抗他的诱惑并继续移动的人。
 关于Sobhraj的杀戮，有义齿和骑士的东西。他和人们公开开放地笑话，关于“肮脏”，易于去的西方旅行者，他们将他的家视为他们的“经验”。他不会喝酒或吸烟，在他们的懒惰上展示他的自律。
 1976年，索布拉河在新德里的Tihar监狱被判处12年。他立即与卫兵和囚犯一起介绍自己。安装了电视，他是美食的食物。
 索布拉河进入他的术语十年，让自己在果阿重新逮捕，从而在监禁中增加了10年。这是一种巧妙的战略，躲避泰国的可能死刑：20年后，荷兰游客谋杀的局限性的规约将到期。到1997年，Sobhraj是免费的，生活在巴黎的郊区。 
要说Sobhraj享受他的臭名昭着是轻描淡写的。他以一种可以让你的皮肤爬行的方式在它中旋转。他给了电视采访，并寻求谈判电影权利。
 到目前为止，赫尔曼住在新西兰。他和Vanessa于1999年落在那里。通过电话，我可以听到他仍然是如何通过Sobhraj的想法进行采访。
 “这是一个Hullabaloo。马戏团。美国人用杀手支付了数千美元的午餐。这对胃来说是一个可怕的事情。“
 然后，2003年，在法国六年后，Sobhraj犯了一个显着的错误计算：他回到尼泊尔。在离开机场的几小时内，他被当地的记者在赌场发现。在索波拉省持续一周之后，记者发表了一份报告，在Hemalyan时代。 Sobhraj立即被捕，并为1975年谋杀康妮丰富的谋杀。
 然而，只有一个问题。判定他的证据是在惠灵顿，坐在赫尔曼车库的各种各样的老家具中。
 Knippenberg在他的惠灵顿家持续多年来的证据盒。照片：维多利亚Birkinshaw。
 谋杀荷兰游客谋杀后30年，在他的外交护照上旅行，赫尔曼已经用他带来了五个纸箱的证据。目击者陈述，登机牌，酒店票据，飞行表现。他将副本给予有关警察局，但担心案件将继续被释放出来，让原来为自己保留。 
在他召唤警报他到2003年的索伯拉克被盗之后，赫尔曼挖了盒子，响了新西兰警察。两位惠灵顿侦探验证了文件，以便提交给互化尼泊尔和加德满都当局的证据。
 赫尔曼的证据是控方的关键。它驳回了Sobhraj声称，在布朗塞希的谋杀时他并不是在尼泊尔。 2004年，Sobhraj在尼泊尔中央监狱的单独监禁中被判处14年。
 一旦赫尔曼进入索布拉谟的主题，他就会难以中断。 1986年，他甚至在使用Sobhraj作为案例研究的领导地位写了一个哈佛博士论文。 “他是黑暗的nietzschean理想 - 一个Übermensch生活，在极端，在VeSuvius的斜坡上，”赫尔曼告诉我。 “一个”有魅力的领导者“不断地证明自己，并相信自己更优于他人。我一直遇到这种形式的虚假权威。无论他们出现在哪里。我拒绝被他迷恋。我不会让他以这种方式渗透我。“
 尽管如此，他坚持认为Sobhraj对他来说不是一个痴迷。 “虽然我担心，直到他或我在一个更美好的世界之前不会结束。他真的出现在我的生活中像疟疾一样，“他说。
 这相当代表了赫尔曼。他是世界上最重要的专家就案子，几乎战略性地解除了对他的抓住。 “为了帮助您在桌子上舒适的同时帮助保护自己的人，”他说。 “这不是我们的责任吗？”
  2013年9月的一个下雨天，赫尔曼在他的门口收到了一封在木制邮箱中的一封信。 “一个非常惠灵顿的信，”他说，笑。 “湿得足以在风中飞灭，但仍然非常可读。”
 “亲爱的Knippenberg，'它开始了。 “我为不邀请写作的写作道歉。” 
这封信来自英国一家作家LED公司的猛犸象。它解释说，他们的一支球队在20世纪70年代一直在尼泊尔旅行，并记住了Charles Sobhraj在那些年份的漫长而可怕的影子。
 “在没有手机或互联网的人们旅行的人们旅行时，这是令人毛骨悚然的，自由轮转时代，”汤姆·肖邦的总监罗克兰在20世纪80年代前往尼泊尔。 “这是如此令人不安，在如此令人惊讶的美丽的地方和图片这样的凄凉和残酷的谋杀案。”
 该公司有兴趣为杀戮展示展示 - 但只有赫尔曼将成为项目的一部分：“如果没有你有意义的参与，我们只会增加猜想和躺在核心的猜想和自信的神秘主义者和猜想与Sobhraj的迷恋。“
  赫尔曼和公司之间安排了一个电话。赫尔曼告诉他们一些自己的电影兴趣 - 奥斯特雷姆东南亚故事的电视适应的Ingmar Bergman电影。
 “他们说'那些东西没有商业上市，”赫尔曼说。 “我告诉他们'哦，是的，我知道。但我已经设法保护了一些“。”
 如果赫尔曼会在演出上工作，那么电影制造商表示，他们可能能够为他提供更多Maugham系列的集。赫曼同意来船上。
 蛇的主要摄影花了一年，由于Covid-19和泰国雨季，拍摄了7个月和挫折。自2014年以来，赫尔曼一直涉及顾问。他靠近摄影记忆派手可友好 - 他能够回顾一下人们坐在房间里的微小细节。基于赫尔曼对卡洛斯的爱，一个关于Carlos The Jackal，其生产设计师Francois Renaud Labarthe的迷你系列，并在蛇上工作。 
该节目在英国突破了突破，占据了20世纪70年代的曼谷，配有镜头和时尚的产品。 在撰写本文时，它是BBC iPlayer最多的节目，拥有3100万个观点。 Tahar Rahim发挥了光滑，冷的Sobhraj，而Jenna Coleman表现了Marie-AndréeLeclerc的复杂性质，Sobhraj的爱情兴趣。 赫尔曼特别兴奋，泰国互联器父亲父亲Sompol Sompol Suthimai的写照特别兴奋。 “我是一个令人沮丧的学术，但Suthimai是一个真正的调查员。 我们总是在这样的漂亮餐厅见面。“  对于赫尔曼来说，在屏幕上看到自己的经验一直是“怀旧和超现实主义的混合”。 Billie Howle扮演年轻的赫尔曼，他的直接态度和热情击败。 赫尔曼认为这很棒。  尽管如此，告诉Sobhraj的故事，一位被警方，记者，编剧和赫尔曼嗨分享的天生问题  ......

Saturday morning on 19 September 2003 was a cool spring day in Wellington. It was the first day of Herman Knippenberg’s retirement and he was looking forward to a breakfast of pancakes. That was when the phone rang. It was a call from a friend in Auckland, informing him that the serial killer Charles Sobhraj had just been arrested in Nepal.
 “Nunc aut nunquam,” Herman says of that day. “It was a now-or-never situation.”
 Herman often says things like this. In his early 70s, tall and personable, his manner is just on the friendly side of brusque. Herman loves a good turn of phrase. He has an unending stock of them in English, Latin and German.
 I first met Herman about a decade ago, when I worked at Unity Books Wellington. Literary conversations are common there, so I wasn’t surprised to hear a Dutch-accented voice over my shoulder one day, asking how many novels by Patrick McGrath we had. He was also a fan of Heinrich Böll. Soon we were swapping titles and looking through the shelves.
 Herman had a list of books he was after and pulled out a notebook with publishers’ names and release dates. If you’re familiar with working retail, you’ll know this isn’t always a good sign. But the topics Herman was tracking were always interesting. He’d pop in and we’d chat about Graham Greene or Carlos the Jackal, or literary crime novels sprinkled with espionage.
 Once, we were informed by a publisher that a book Herman had ordered was going to arrive a few days late, and I got my first clue that Herman can be uncommonly determined. A man who does not accept no easily. “It’s not about what they’re telling you,” Herman said, convinced that there must be some way to get the book promptly. “It’s about what you think!”
 We became friends. I would go for afternoon tea at his home. There’s a white picket-fence outside, comfy chairs by the window and always a good variety of biscuits. I learned that Herman had been a career diplomat for the Dutch government. He’d lived in Athens, Luxembourg, Vienna, New York, Jakarta and Thailand. In 1987, he met his second wife Vanessa — a Kiwi — in Vienna. Also a career diplomat, and just as animated as Herman, she was posted to the New Zealand Embassy at the time.
 There was something about Herman that wasn’t everyday. Sometimes he’d say things like, “As you may know, I was in Bangkok at that time.” Or, “Because of my involvement with the case.” Herman himself never dropped me in the deep end of his life story. Which I appreciate. It was a colleague at the bookstore who mentioned Herman had his own section of a Wikipedia page. It was only slowly, one clue leading to another, that I learned that Herman once helped to catch a serial killer.
 “The good news is that your intelligence level is high,” Herman was told in a psychological evaluation. “The bad news may be that you experience problems with authority from people you can’t respect.”
 For a time in the 1970s, Charles Sobhraj was one of the most wanted men in Asia. Police in multiple countries had tried and failed to apprehend this sophisticated conman for his lurid despatchings of young travellers on the Hippie Trail. In the media, he was variously known as The Bikini Killer, The Splitter and The Serpent, nicknames inspired by his grisly methods and slippery evasions. By the time of his capture, Sobhraj had variously poisoned, drowned, strangled, stabbed and burnt 12 people. At least 12 people.
 There have been two films, four documentaries, and several books about Sobhraj over the years — a perennially charming monster, he almost seems custom-made for crime thrillers. Netflix is now screening  The Serpent, an eight-episode BBC co-production that, for the first time, highlights Herman’s critical role in apprehending Sobhraj.
 Stories about serial killers are a dime-a-dozen. Stories about the real people who catch them are rarer. When I asked Herman to tell me about his role in the case, he was characteristically prepared.
 Herman can cite every article on Sobhraj in detail, with the fervour of a young lawyer or investigative journalist. “We will make a piece that can withstand scrutiny,” he declares. He frequently backtracks and qualifies his statements for factual precision.
 Over a series of phone calls, he becomes increasingly invested in this article — not just in its accuracy, but in how his tale is told. He has a suggestion for a title, and an idea for how the story should start. He insists we recognise the Canadian Ambassador in Thailand, a figure almost no book or article has mentioned. (“Bill Bauer. Don’t forget how important he was. He was formidable. A pillar of the west.”)
 At the start of our first conversation, I can hear papers being rearranged in the background.
 “I’m ready for it. Shoot. I have everything on hand. Personally, I think we should start with the day Sobhraj came back into my life. The day he was arrested in Kathmandu. That’s when the New Zealand Police became involved. I remember it clearly.”
  “Yes. It was a Saturday morning on the 19th of September 2003. A cool spring day in Wellington. It was the first day of my retirement and I was looking forward to a breakfast of pancakes. That’s when the phone rang.”
  Herman Knippenberg was born in Breukhuizen, a small town in the Netherlands close to the German border. His father was a doctor and a member of the Dutch Resistance. Tiger tanks would roll through town and SS officers would knock on their door to ask his father’s whereabouts. In October 1944, when Herman was six months old, his family home was destroyed, likely by an Allied bomb dropped in the Battle of Overloon. His mother put him in a pram and walked 50 kilometres to his grandparents’ house. That’s where Herman was raised.
 Herman consistently graduated top of his class, but from his very earliest years, he liked to do things in his own way. “I was always a difficult child,” he says. “My mother would give an instruction and I would do the opposite. I would not take issue — I would just do it very differently. Between three and five I would correct anyone making a mistake reading my favourite books to me. ‘No, that’s not what the author said,’ I would say. I would let them know!”
 At 19, after hitchhiking through Syria and Turkey for three months and spending two months in Egypt, Herman applied to the Diplomatic Service. “In my psychological testing they said, ‘The good news isthat your intelligence level is high’,” he recalls. “‘The bad news may be that you experience problems with authority from people you can’t respect.’”
 In 1976, aged 32 and still early in his career, Herman was working as a junior diplomatic officer at the Dutch Embassy in Bangkok when he was asked to make inquiries about two Dutch backpackers who’d been traveling in Thailand: Henk Bintanja and his girlfriend Cornelia Hemker. Their families were worried: there had been no letters home for six weeks — not for Christmas or the birthday of Cornelia’s mother and sister. Based on his own experience as a backpacker, Herman’s intuition told him something was up. He felt propelled by kinship as well as duty.
  The couple’s last contact mentioned a Frenchman in Hong Kong who’d sold them a gemstone at a low price and invited them to join him in Thailand. Herman learned that Henk and Cornelia had never reached the hotel that they had listed on their arrival card. He also knew that a few months earlier, the bodies of two tourists, supposedly Australian, had been found in Bangkok. There had been articles in the local press but no one, including the Thai police, had any leads.
 “As they say in the Netherlands, they had heard the ringing of the bell but did not yet know where the clapper hangs.”
 A detail caught Herman’s attention: one of the dead tourists had been wearing a t-shirt made in Holland. He contacted the Australian Embassy and was told that the two Australians who were originally suspected of being the victims were still alive. So Herman obtained the missing Dutch tourists’ dental records and used them to confirm that they belonged to the burnt remains sitting in a Bangkok morgue. Herman himself went to identify the bodies. “I will never forget it,” he says.
 In theory, this is where Herman’s role as a diplomatic officer should have ended as the Thai police took over. But Herman doubted the police were sufficiently motivated and so he stayed on the case.
 Once he started to look into the matter, he heard rumours of other suspicious backpacker killings in Thailand, as if there was a bogeyman stalking the Hippie Trail. A young woman named Teresa Knowlton had been drugged and abducted and then dressed in a bikini and left on a beach. “Knowlton was off to become a nun you know!” Herman says. When he first told me this, I wasn’t sure whether to believe him. The body of another young traveller, Vitali Hakim, had been found burnt on the side of the road.
 Not long after the Dutch tourists were identified, a man and woman had used the victims’ passports to fly to Nepal. The couple returned to Thailand five days later on the passports of two more backpackers, American Connie Bronzich and her Canadian friend Laurent Carriere. It would emerge that Bronzich and Carriere had also been murdered: stabbed multiple times and charred beyond recognition.
 Herman’s investigations were pulling at every aspect of his own life. His first wife Angela was supportive and helped with the research, but Herman was increasingly on edge. His superiors at the embassy were growing frustrated that he was apparently more focused on chasing a murderer than carrying out his administrative duties.
 Via a good friend at the Belgian Embassy, Herman heard of a suspicious character named Alan Gaultier. He also got word of an apartment where the Dutch couple might have stayed. It had a reputation as a frequent site for parties of travellers, hosted by a mysterious Frenchman who was also named Alan Gaultier. Neighbours in the building had reported screams.
 As it turned out, the host of the parties at the apartment was a different Alan Gaultier. But, improbably, the link had led Herman to the crime scene.
 “I couldn’t believe the coincidence. It was a deus ex machina. Like something in a cheap airplane novel!”
 Herman was given permission by Thai police to enter the empty apartment. He assembled an action team — himself, his Belgian friend, an American Embassy contact and one of the neighbours. Inside, they found 12 Western passports and large plastic tubs of sedatives and syringes. Herman recalls seeing scratch marks on a bedpost that he’d later realise were made by victims handcuffed to the bed.
 Herman took his findings to Thai police and pressured them to make an arrest. But when confronted, “Alan Gaultier” produced a forged US passport in the name of David Alan Gore. This deception got him out of Thailand.
 His name, of course, was not Alan Gaultier or David Alan Gore but Charles Sobhraj. By this point, he knew the authorities were on his trail — and became even more extravagantly violent. He next popped up in India and travelled on the passport of an Israeli tourist who had gone missing there. He also fatally poisoned a French man named Jean-Luc Solomon. The Indian police were alerted. Then, in July 1976, with two new accomplices, Sohraj insinuated himself as a local guide for a French tour group of 22 students in New Delhi. He offered them anti-dysentery medication and intentionally overdid the dosage. As the victims stumbled around the lobby of the Vikram Hotel, he was held in the bar by three of the party until the Indian police arrived and arrested him.
 Herman had just returned from a fishing trip when he received a call from the Canadian Ambassador, Bill Bauer. “The Indian police have also been fishing,” Bauer informed him. “And you won’t believe what they’ve caught!”
 The French tour group survived, but Sobrhaj was tried for the murder of Jean-Luc Solomon. This was how his full identity finally came to light.
 To say Sobhraj enjoyed his notoriety is an understatement. He revelled in it in a way that makes your skin crawl.
 Of Indian-Vietnamese descent, Charles Sobhraj was born in Saigon. He was partly raised by a French Army stepfather and ferried between Indochina and France as a child. Imprisoned for petty crimes as a teen, he bribed guards to make his cell more comfortable. After his release, he headed for Southeast Asia with an entourage that included his younger brother André and various girlfriends and accomplices. They established a pattern of robbing jewellery stores, being caught, feigning illness and escaping jail.
 Sobhraj cut a debonair figure in Bangkok, equally comfortable in society and the backstreets. He spoke five languages. He sported tinted sunglasses and berets, turtlenecks and sports jackets. Passports were his currency, identities his objective. He inveigled himself into travellers’ lives with a rock-star swagger. He was capable of organising anything; gems, drugs, a bed to sleep in. His victims were usually those who attempted to resist his lure and keep moving.
 There is something both righteous and cavalier about Sobhraj’s killings. He’d openly joke with people about “dirty”, easy-going Western travellers who treated his home as their “experience”. He wouldn’t drink or smoke himself, showboating his self-discipline over their indolence.
 In 1976, Sobhraj was sentenced to 12 years at Tihar Prison in New Delhi. He immediately ingratiated himself with the guards and prisoners. A TV was installed and he was served gourmet food.
 Ten years into his term, Sobhraj escaped and let himself be re-arrested in Goa, thereby adding another 10 years to his imprisonment. It was an ingenious strategy to dodge a likely death sentence in Thailand: after 20 years, the statute of limitations on the murder of the Dutch tourists would expire. By 1997, Sobhraj was free and living in the suburbs of Paris.
 To say Sobhraj enjoyed his notoriety is an understatement. He revelled in it in a way that can make your skin crawl. He gave TV interviews and sought to negotiate film rights.
 By this point, Herman was living in New Zealand. He and Vanessa had settled there in 1999. Over the phone, I can hear how galled he still is by the idea of Sobhraj giving interviews for cash.
 “It was a hullabaloo. A circus. Americans paying thousands of dollars to lunch with a killer. It was a terrible thing to stomach.”
 And then, in 2003, after six years in France, Sobhraj made a remarkable miscalculation: he returned to Nepal. Within hours of leaving the airport he was spotted at a casino by a local journalist. After following Sobhraj for a fortnight, the journalist published a report with photographs in  The Himalyan Times. Sobhraj was immediately arrested and charged for the 1975 murder of Connie Bronzich.
 There was just one problem, however. The evidence to convict him was in Wellington, sitting among an assortment of old furniture in Herman’s garage.
 Boxes of evidence that Knippenberg kept for years in his Wellington home. Photo: Victoria Birkinshaw.
 For 30 years after the murder of the Dutch tourists, travelling the world on his diplomatic passport, Herman had taken five cardboard boxes of evidence with him. Witness statements, embarkation cards, hotel bills, flight manifests. He’d given copies to the relevant police authorities, but, fearing the case would continue to be brushed aside, kept the originals for himself.
 After he got the call alerting him to Sobhraj’s arrest in 2003, Herman dug the boxes out and rang the New Zealand police. Two Wellington detectives verified the documents so the evidence could be submitted to Interpol Nepal and the Kathmandu authorities.
 Herman’s evidence was key to the prosecution. It refuted Sobhraj’s claim that he wasn’t in Nepal at the time of Bronzich’s murder. In 2004, Sobhraj was sentenced to 14 years in solitary confinement in Nepal’s Central Prison.
 Once Herman gets going on the subject of Sobhraj,he can be hard to interrupt. In 1986, he even wrote a Harvard PhD dissertation on leadership that uses Sobhraj as a case study. “He is that dark Nietzschean ideal — an Übermensch living, in extremis, on the slopes of Vesuvius,” Herman tells me. “A ‘charismatic leader’ constantly having to prove himself, believing himself innately superior to others. I have always had a problem with such forms of false authority. Wherever they appear. I refuse to be enamoured by him. I won’t let him pervade me in that way.”
 Still, he maintains that Sobhraj is not an obsession for him. “Though I fear it will not be over until he or I is in a better world. He really comes in and out of my life like malaria,” he says.
 This rather typifies Herman. He’s both the world’s foremost expert on the case and almost strategically dismissive of its hold over him. “To help safeguard your own people while you are sitting comfortably at a desk,” he says. “Was that not our responsibility?”
  One rainy day  in September 2013, Herman received a letter in the wooden mailbox at his gate. “A very Wellington letter,” he says, laughing. “Wet enough not to fly off in the wind, but still quite readable.”
 “‘Dear Mr Knippenberg,’ it began. ‘I apologise writing to you uninvited.’”
 The letter was from Mammoth Productions, a writer-led company in the UK. It explained that one of their team had been travelling through Nepal in the 1970s and remembered the long and frightening shadow cast by Charles Sobhraj in those years.
 “It was that heady, free-wheeling era when people travelled without mobile phones or the internet,” recalls director Tom Shankland, who travelled to Nepal in the 1980s. “It was so unsettling to be in such an astonishingly beautiful place and picture such bleak and brutal murders.”
 The company was interested in making a show about the killings — but only if Herman would be part of the project: “Without your meaningful involvement we are only adding to the conjecture and the self-serving mystique and conjecture that lies at the heart of the fascination with Sobhraj.”
  A call was arranged between Herman and the company. Herman told them about some of his own cinematic interests — the films of Ingmar Bergman, television adaptations of the Southeast Asian stories of Somerset Maugham.
 “They said ‘That stuff is not available commercially’,” Herman says. “I told them ‘Oh yes, I know. But I have managed to secure some’.”
 If Herman would work on the show, the filmmakers said, they might be able to provide him some more episodes of the Maugham series. Herman agreed to come on board.
 Main photography on  The Serpent took one year, with filming over seven months and setbacks due to Covid-19 and the Thai rainy season. Herman has been involved as an advisor since 2014. His near photographic memory has come in handy — he is able to recall minute details such as where people were sitting in a room. Based on Herman’s love of Carlos, a mini-series about Carlos the Jackal, its production designer, Francois Renaud Labarthe, was contracted to work on  The Serpent.
 The show has been a breakout hit in the UK, capturing 1970s Bangkok with a mix of found footage and stylish production. At the time of writing, it’s the most-watched show on BBC iPlayer, with 31 million views. Tahar Rahim plays a smooth, cold version of Sobhraj, while Jenna Coleman performs the complex character of Marie-Andrée LeClerc, Sobhraj’s love interest. Herman was especially excited by the portrayal of Lieutenant Colonel Sompol Suthimai of Interpol in Thailand. “I was a frustrated academic at best, but Suthimai was a true investigator. And we always met in such nice restaurants.”
 For Herman, the experience of seeing himself on screen has been “a mix of the nostalgic and the surreal”. Billie Howle, who plays the young Herman, has his forthright manner and zeal down pat. Herman thinks it’s brilliant.
 Still, there is an innate problem to telling Sobhraj’s story, one shared by police, journalists, screenwriters and Herman hi
......

note_en:
Earlier today, Microsoft offered us a  peek at Visual Studio 2022, which will offer its first public preview builds later this summer. If you're into the Visual Studio ecosystem, this looks like a killer upgrade.
  With Visual Studio 2022, you'll finally be able to take advantage of all of your system RAM. Earlier versions of Visual Studio are 32-bit applications, thereby hobbling VS to a maximum of 2GiB RAM.
 The new VS2022 is fully 64-bit—without which the first GIF in the gallery above wouldn't be able to open a whopping 1,600 projects and roughly 300,000 files at once.
  Visual Studio's AI IntelliCode Engine—which the less-fancy among us might simply think of as "command autocomplete"—gets an upgrade as well.
 We don't have any specific details on what's different, just a promise that "Visual Studio 2022 will provide more and deeper integrations into your daily workflows, helping you to take the right action in the right place at the right time."
        The new Visual Studio 2022 offers full support for .NET 6 and all the features it offers. This includes  .NET MAUI—the Multi-platform App UI visible in the gallery above—as well as .NET Hot Reload, a feature allowing developers to apply code updates to a running app, without needing to restart the app or lose its current state.
 Visual C++ also gets new productivity features, tooling, and IntelliSense. Along with these features, cross-platform devs get support for CMake, Linux, and Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL).
   This includes built-in logic and checkpoints to guide devs through merge and review processes, anticipating feedback from colleagues ahead of time. Microsoft says "our guiding principle here was helping you to have higher confidence in the code you deliver."
  If you're a developer in the Visual Studio ecosystem—or considering becoming one—check out the full Visual Studio 2022  announcement for even more details, including improved code search, Azure support, a Mac platform refresh, and more.
title: Microsoft Visual Studio 2022将（最终）输入64位世界
title_en: Microsoft Visual Studio 2022 will (finally) enter the 64-bit world

由于诸如Elon Musk的Starlink等低轨道互联网提供商，请开始测试其服务，安全分析师承认潜在的中断，这些系统可能导致专权威权制度，因为人们开始访问开放和未经审查的互联网。在俄罗斯，中国和朝鲜等国家，在信息主导和控制对于制度生存至关重要的地方，低轨道互联网卫星可能会产生重大挑战。
 “我认为这些卫星通过提供低成本或通过这些卫星系统，兰德的高级政策分析师Michael Schwille迈克尔·施维尔（Michael Schwille）提供了一位高级政策分析师，为民主国家为民主国家提供了一点，使民主国家为授权制度转向授权制度的机会。 ，告诉汇报。
 伊朗，俄罗斯和中国等国家分配了重要的资源和精力，以控制信息如何在各自的国家传播。人们，尤其是司法员，试图进入开放互联网并在这些国家内部难以沟通。
 这些低轨道互联网解决方案对独裁者提出了问题，因为国家监管机构无法控制它们，并且他们可以在地球最偏远的地区提供互联网。
  Starlink Ceo Elon Musk于2016年告诉记者，中国及其互联网法对其公司来说是一个很大的关注点。
 “如果他们对我们感到不安，他们可以吹卫星，这不会很好。中国可以这样做。所以我们可能不会在那里播出，“他在2016年的采访中说明了。最近中国表示，他们将推出自己的10,000个低轨道互联网卫星的星座，称为Starnet与竞争对手的星际思。
 俄罗斯已经开始考虑法律来制作明星非法。俄罗斯政府表示，如果抓住使用该服务，则将其单位用户提供10,000至30,000卢布（约200美元400美元）和较大的实体，较大的实体（约有12,000美元）卢布。 
2020年8月，俄罗斯的航天署长德米特里罗戈辛表示，Starlink是“一个相当掠夺，聪明，强大，美国的高科技政策，它使用震惊和敬畏，以便在所有人的军事利益之前推进。” Rogozin公开表示Starlink的更多人道主义方面，因为它将提供与生活在偏远地区的人们“废话”的互联网接入。
 像伊朗和朝鲜一样，监测和审查他们的互联网的其他国家，对卫星互联网服务的声音较少。然而，几乎没有理由相信他们对开放互联网的想法不那么温暖。
  这些低轨道卫星相对靠近地球，与地面只有几百公里，相比大约35,000公里左右的大型电信卫星相比。这允许终端进入这些近地卫星以非常小和便携。例如，星形丝盘是直径约为19英寸或大约一个超大披萨的大小，它只会重量几磅。
    根据施威尔的说法，这使得它们易于走私，设置，快速拆除。这些小的“互联网上的箱式”系统不需要房地产，并且他们需要一个简单的电源来运行。
 最终，用户可以快速绕过任何州的Internet控件并获得开放网的访问是可行的。此外，用户可以通过互联网或通过聊天服务来拨打电话，知道它们的对话未被跟踪或监视。
 “我认为，在民主国家工具套件中获取信息作为一个强大的工具，”施维尔解释道。 “对于太久，我们通过针对美国机构和群体的专制赞助攻击，我们一直受到针对性的消毒竞选活动的攻击。”
 互联网在民主国家的内在问题是，大多数是不融入的。在合理的开放环境中存在思想，并且错误信息和欺骗可以很快传播。因此，开放的互联网可以通过他们的对手反对民主国家。例如，俄罗斯能够轻松影响2016年美国总统选举和Brexit活动的结果。分析师称为“民主的困境”。关键问题是互联网的开放性可以武装化以侵蚀民主基础。 
然而，施维尔指出了像Starlink这样的系统可能是民主国家反击的好方法。 “威权主义最害怕的是进入未过滤的信息，”他告诉汇报。
 施韦尔指出“独裁者的困境”，声称授权制度不希望“非政权叙述”到达其公民，也不希望在批准和监控渠道之外发生任何沟通。
 施维尔表示，全世界的民主国家可以“谈谈”这些低轨道卫星互联网选项，并创造一个全球叙述。正如那种叙述到授权国家的人，那么政权将被迫采取行动，将额外的时间和精力分配给另一个战斗，他们可能不一定有战争胸部斗争。或者，他们可能被迫与互联网服务更加开放。
   民主国家可以为专制国家的目标潜在用户创造消息传递，施维尔设想了促进概念的叙述。
 “”看看现在互联网现在将在美国的所有部分提供，从阿巴拉契亚山脉到阿拉斯加的荒野。现在，所有美国人都可以廉价获取信息，无论他们住在哪里，＆＃39;“他的Adlibbed营销音高表示。随着这条消息传播，世界各地的人将开始注意到。
   “他们也可以在整个开发社区谈论。施韦尔解释说，如何以减少的价格向撒哈拉以南非洲提供[互联网服务]。“ “你植入了任何人都可以使用这种技术的想法。独裁者是否有隐含的威胁？我会说是的。“
 在偏远地区可访问的低成本互联网服务将成为一个非常引人注目的叙述，随着施维尔解释，发展有效的“潜在的威胁”，尊敬的政权将必须处理。 
此外，西方民主国家为信息战而闻名。在冷战中，打字机和施乐机被走私到东欧，以帮助增加未经审查信息的传播。
 “走私生产，复制和传播信息都是充分的记录，”施维尔表达了。简单地说，这些卫星系统足够小，以至于在边界中走私了数百个将是直截了当的。施维尔指出，任何这种行为都会升级与政权的紧张局势。
 “虽然你可以在这些卫星系统中流门并通过持不同政见者来传递它们，但我们争论更细致的方法，”他得出结论。
 这些设备的“威胁”进入制度的边界和传播的自由互联网的叙述将足以帮助利用对抗的权力。奇怪的是，它将是基于主动虚假或欺骗的战争，而是只要提供信息的访问。
    最终，可以使这些系统用于积极措施。从一个智力收集的角度来看，资产，操作员或甚至在制度内部的持不同审议者可以迅速使用这些系统来获取世界其他地区的信息，或者使用它来传播政权内的特定信息。此外，黑客可以使用这些系统在网络攻击的独裁者的边界内运行。
 施威尔认为，这一切都归结为斯堪克斯等民主国家和公司是否共同使用这项技术来挑战专制领导者。为了通过积极的欺骗来平衡操纵自由互联网以侵蚀民主理想的制度的尺度，民主国家可以通过洪水洪水的洪水侵犯互联网来反击。

note_en:
As low-orbit satellite internet providers, such as Elon Musk’s Starlink, begin testing their services, security analysts   recognize  the potential disruption these systems could cause to authoritarian regimes as people start accessing an open and uncensored internet. In countries like Russia, China, and North Korea, where information dominance and control are essential to the regime’s survival, low-orbit internet  satellites could pose a significant challenge.
 “I think that these satellites provide an   opportunity  for democracies to turn the tables a bit on authoritarian regimes by providing low cost or free access to information via these satellite systems,” Michael Schwille, a senior policy analyst at RAND who has expertise in information warfare, told   The Debrief.
 Countries like Iran,  Russia, and  China allocate significant resources and energy to control how information disseminates across their respective nations. People, especially dissidents, attempting to access the open internet and communicate across that internet is difficult inside those countries.
 These low-orbit internet solutions pose a problem for dictators because state regulators can’t control them, and they can offer internet in the most remote areas of the planet.
  Starlink CEO Elon Musk told reporters in 2016 that China and its internet laws were a big concern for his company.
 “If they get upset with us, they can blow our satellites up, which wouldn’t be good. China can do that. So probably we shouldn’t broadcast there,” he stated in a 2016 interview. Recently China has   said  they will launch their own constellation of 10,000 low-orbit internet satellites called StarNet to rival Starlink.
 Russia has already begun contemplating   laws  to make Starlink illegal. The Russian government stated it would fine individual users 10,000 to 30,000 rubles (about $200-$400 USD) and larger entities close to a million (around $12,000 USD) rubles if caught using the service.
    Russia’s space chief Dmitry Rogozin, in   August  of 2020, said that Starlink is “a rather predatory, clever, powerful, high-technology policy of the USA, which uses Shock and Awe in order to advance, before all, their military interests.” Rogozin publicly stated the more humanitarian aspects of Starlink, in that it would provide internet access to people living in remote areas, “nonsense.”
 Like Iran and North Korea, other nations, who monitor and censor their internet, have been less vocal concerning satellite internet services. However, there is little reason to believe they would be any less warm to the idea of an open internet.
  These low-orbit satellites are relatively close to the Earth, and only a few hundred kilometers from the surface, compared to large telecommunications satellites that sit around 35,000 kilometers away. This allows for the terminals to access these near-Earth satellites to be pretty small and portable. A Starlink dish, for example, is about 19 inches in diameter or about the size of an extra-large pizza, and it only weighs a couple of pounds.
    According to Schwille, this makes them easy to smuggle, set up, and tear down quickly. These small ‘internet-in-a-box’ systems require no real estate, and they need a simple electrical source to operate.
 Ultimately, it is feasible for users to quickly bypass any state’s internet controls and gain access to an open web. Moreover, users could place calls over the internet or via chat services, knowing that their conversation is not being tracked or monitored.
 “I see that access to information as a powerful tool in a democracies tool kit,” Schwille explained. “For too long, we have been getting hammered by authoritarian-sponsored attacks on U.S. institutions and populations via targeted disinformation campaigns.”
 The inherent problem with the internet in democratic nations is that most of it is unfiltered. Ideas exist in a reasonably open environment, and misinformation and disinformation can be quickly spread. The open internet can therefore be used against democratic states by their adversaries.  For example, Russia was able to easily influence the results of the   2016 U.S. Presidential election  and the   Brexit campaign . Analysts have called this   “democracy’s dilemma.”  The critical issue is that the very openness of the internet can be weaponized to erode the foundations of democracy.
 However, Schwille points out that systems like Starlink may be a good way for democratic states to fight back. “What authoritarians fear most is access to unfiltered information,” he told   The Debrief.
 Schwille points to the “  Dictator’s Dilemma ” and asserts that authoritarian regimes do not want “non-regime narratives” reaching their citizens, nor do they want any communication to occur outside of approved and monitored channels.
 Schwille suggests that democracies worldwide could “talk up” these low-orbit satellite internet options and create a global narrative. As that narrative reaches people in authoritarian states, the regimes will be forced to take action, allocating additional time and energy to another battle they may not necessarily have the war chest to fight. Or, they may be forced to be more open with their internet services.
   Democracies could create messaging to target potential users in authoritarian states, and Schwille envisions a narrative that promotes the concept.
 “‘Look how the internet will now be provided across all parts of the U.S., from the Appalachian mountains to the Alaskan wilderness. Now all Americans can have cheap access to information, no matter where they live,'” his adlibbed marketing pitch stated. As this message spreads, people all over the world will begin to take notice.
   “They could also talk it up throughout the development community. How [internet service] could be provided to sub-Saharan Africa at reduced prices,” Schwille explained. “You plant the idea that this technology can be used anywhere, by anybody. Is there an implied threat there for dictators? I would say yes.”
 A low-cost internet service accessible in remote areas would make a very compelling narrative and, as Schwille explains, develop a potent “underlying threat” an authoritarian regime would have to deal with.
 Moreover, Western democracies are well known for information warfare. During the Cold War, typewriters and Xerox machines were   smuggled  into Eastern Europe to help increase the dissemination of uncensored information.
 “Smuggling in capabilities to produce, replicate and disseminate information are all well documented,” Schwille expressed. Simply put, these satellite systems are small enough that smuggling hundreds of them across a border would be straightforward. Schwille noted that any such act would escalate tensions with the regime.
 “While you could smuggle in these satellite systems and pass them around for use by dissidents, we argue for a more nuanced approach,” he concluded.
 The “threat” of these devices entering a regime’s borders and the spreading ‘free internet’ narrative would be enough to help leverage power against an adversary. Oddly enough, it would be warfare based not on active disinformation or deception but on simply providing access to information.
    Ultimately, it is possible to have these systems used in   active  measures. From an intelligence-gathering standpoint, assets, operatives, or even dissidents inside a regime could quickly use these systems to get information out to the rest of the world or use it to spread specific information inside the regime. Moreover, hackers could use these systems to operate within a dictator’s borders in cyber attacks.
 Schwille argues that this all boils down to whether democratic nations and companies like SpaceX will work together to use this technology to challenge authoritarian leaders. In an attempt to balance the scales against regimes who manipulate the free internet to erode democratic ideals via active disinformation, democracies could fight back by flooding authoritarian states with access to the very internet they weaponize.

You can also get all the latest  news and exciting feature content from The Debrief on    Flipboard  , and    Pinterest  . 在2019年5月宣布的沙箱计划下进行测试 - 将政府资金和监管支持指向R＆amp; D在无人机空间中 - 最初通过虚拟测试，例如避免和检测系统。
 Sees.ai是一个早期参与者在沙箱中，现在已经授权在三（物理）网站上试验一个例行BVLOS操作的概念，而无需预授权每种航班。
  TechStars支持的启动专注于工业设施中的无人机操作 - 建立技术，以扩展旱机的使用进行检查和维护，如石油＆amp;天然气部门通过使飞行员能够从中心位置启用遥控器工艺，而不是每次飞行都需要在现场。
 但它很清楚，BVLOS功能对于无人机技术的其他用途至关重要 - 例如交付 - 因此CAA呼叫试验“无人机行业的重要一步”。
 “通过测试工业环境中的概念进行检查，监测和维护目的，旨在在最初的情况下证明其系统在此背景下的安全性，然后在将其延伸到随着时间的推移越来越挑战的任务之前，”它补充说。
 根据当前的U.K.规则，无人机运营商必须在视线中保持飞机，并遵循该国的无人机代码 - 除非他们有特定的权限。
 以前获得了此类许可的一家公司是美国科技巨头亚马逊 -  2016年开始测试英国的BVLOS送货无人机 - 并继续致力于将商业无人机送货服务推向市场，以其主要的空中品牌。 
亚马逊的努力已经在制作中已经多年了（自2013年以来一直在运行实验） - 据报道，去年涉及主要空气来源的FT仍然仍然仍然是“岁月”，从而实现了在规模上的无人机交付的目标。因此，虽然（另一个）U.K.审判BVLOS无人机技术被称为监管机构的一个重要发展，任何预计在野外的无人机即将发货的任何精神可能会感到失望。
 CAA授权的SEES.AI试验将使BVLOS测试航班在150英尺下运行 - 最初要求观察者与飞机保持在视觉视线中，并在每个调节器的情况下能够与远程导频通信。
 所以，从技术上，试验将开始作为延长视线（EVLOS），这仍然需要限制VS True BVLO  - 从远程飞行员（通过部署飞行观察者）来实现无人机飞行以进一步的操作，但未删除完全是现场观察者，终极行业目标也是如此。更新：CAA确认意图是删除观察者一次/如果试验证明概念有效。它还表示，试验与传统的EVLOS飞行不同，因为观察者不需要与飞行员持续接触 - 如果需要，他们只需要能够联系飞行员。
 在上秋季发布的监管路线图中，CAA写道，在非隔离的空域中，需要许多步骤才能到达BVLOS“通常”的“业务”的态势 - 因此在商业无人机之前仍然看起来仍然是一条漫长的道路将能够在远离循环中的任何人类的数据（或提供的东西）周围合法地。
 “运营商的长期愿望是为BVLOS运营成为英国跨越商业的常规部分。此愿景需要大量的证据，经验和学习所涉及的每个人。 CAA路线图说明，将不可避免地需要创新，测试，学习和重复，以便朝着愿景工作，“CAA路线图说明。
 首席执行官John McKenna在一份声明中发表了一篇声明的审判授权，它补充说：“我们正在加速朝着无人机在规模上自主飞行的未来 - 高涨，在我们的工业遗迹中高涨，郊区和城市。确保这个英国第一许可是这一旅程的重要一步，这将为公共卫生＆amp的社会带来大益处;安全，效率和环境影响。“
note_en:
The test is taking place under a sandbox program announced back in May 2019 — directing government funding and regulatory support to R&D in the drone space — initially through virtual testing, such as of avoid and detect systems.
 Sees.ai,  an early participant in the sandbox, has now secured authorization to trial a concept for routine BVLOS operations at three (physical) sites without needing to pre-authorise each flight.
  The  Techstars-backed startup is focused on drone operations in industrial settings — building tech to scale the use of drones for inspection and maintenance purposes in industries, such as the oil & gas sector, by enabling pilots to remote-control craft from a central location, rather than needing to be on site for each flight.
 But it’s clear BVLOS capabilities will be essential for other uses of drone tech — such as delivery — hence the CAA calling the trial “a significant step forward for the drone industry”.
 “By testing the concept in industrial environments for inspection, monitoring and maintenance purposes, sees.ai aims to prove the safety of its system within this context initially, before extending it to address increasingly challenging missions over time,” it added.
 Under current U.K. rules, drone operators must keep their aircraft within line of sight and follow the country’s  drone code — unless they have specific permissions to do otherwise.
 One company that previously gained such permission was U.S. tech giant Amazon — which started testing BVLOS delivery drones in the UK back in  2016 — and continues to work on bringing a commercial drone delivery service to market, under its Prime Air brand.
 Amazon’s effort has already been years in the making ( it’s been running experiments since 2013) — and last year the  FT, citing a Prime Air source, reported that it still remains “years” out from realizing the goal of drone deliveries at scale. So while (another) U.K. trial of BVLOS drone tech is being lauded as a significant development for the industry by the regulator, any Brits expecting drone deliveries in the wild anytime soon are likely to be disappointed.
 The CAA authorization for the sees.ai trial will enable the BVLOS test flights to operate under 150ft — initially requiring an observer to remain in visual line of sight with the aircraft and be able to communicate with the remote pilot if necessary, per the regulator.
 So, technically then, the trials will begin as extended-line-of-sight (EVLOS), which still entail limits vs true BVLOS — enabling drone flights to operate further than 500m from the remote pilot (by deploying flight observers) but not removing on-site observers entirely, as is the ultimate industry goal.  Update: The CAA confirmed the intention is to remove the observer once/if the trials prove the concept works. It also said the trial differs from traditional EVLOS flying because the spotter does not need to be in constant contact with the pilot — they only need the ability to contact the pilot if required.
 In a  regulatory roadmap published last fall the CAA wrote that many steps are required to arrive at the sought-for situation of BVLOS being ‘business as usual’ in non-segregated airspace — so there still looks to be a long road ahead before commercial drones will be able to legally whiz around gathering data (or delivering stuff) far from any humans in the loop.
 “The long-term aspiration of operators is for BVLOS operations to be a routine part of business across the UK. This vision requires a significant volume of evidence, experience and learning by everyone involved. There will inevitably be a need for innovators and the CAA to build, test, learn and repeat in small steps to work towards the vision,” the CAA roadmap notes.
 Commenting on sees.ai’s trial authorization in a statement, CEO John McKenna dubbed it a “significant milestone”, adding: “We are accelerating towards a future where drones fly autonomously at scale — high up alongside manned aviation and low down inside our industrial sites, suburbs and cities. Securing this UK-first permission is a major step on this journey which will deliver big benefits to society across public health & safety, efficiency and environmental impact.”

进入这一领域已经来到欧洲启动戳屋，这是有效地将“戳碗”的生产用于食品交付平台。 Poke House专门从事碗食物，这些食物通常包括腌制鱼，粘性米饭，泡菜，面条等。
 该公司现已筹集了2000万欧元（2400万美元），由欧洲州欧洲夏罗首都领导的B系列资金，米兰投资合作伙伴SGR支持FG2资本和再投资。它使用技术和数据来通过优雅的所有食品交付平台，优化其产品的生产和交付，如优步等。意大利出生的食品科技创业公司声称在两年内建立了“100亿欧元+公司”。
  Poke House于意大利，葡萄牙和西班牙开设了30多家商店，由Matteo Pichi和Vittoria Zanetti创立，现在拥有400名员工。它宣称2021年的预期营业额为40亿欧元。
 通过资金，启动将开始在现有市场开设新商店，进入法国，并在英国的扩展开始。
 Poke House表示，它在后端使用了很多技术，跟踪供应链的每个元素以优化业务。它还分析了来自第三方交付平台的数据（即送货，加洛沃，Ubereats）来提供10分钟的食品准备时间，并在25分钟以下的交货时间。
 Poke House的联合创始人Matteo Pichi表示：“大流行已经挑战了我们的食品行业，我们认为技术是创新和数字化传统餐厅体验的前进方向。我们在快速但健康的食物中看到人们欲望的转变。 Poke Bowls适合这种新的需求，它促进了附近提供的快速健康的食物选择更加平衡，积极和可持续的生活方式。“
 PICHI谈到TechCrunch：“我们的竞争对手是伟大的健康概念，如美好的甜蜜或甜甜素。但同时，我们认为我们很幸运，因为我们真的是来自食品交付专家或前员工100％的第一个品牌之一。我们的下一个竞争对手将在数据分析和数字品牌建设中充满了本土虚拟品牌。我们使用食物交付平台作为媒体平台，我们比渠道中的竞争对手更重。“ 
欧拉首都的创始合伙人Gianfranco Burei表示：“Poke House Business Model乘坐一些主要趋势（食品科技，健康食品，交货，定制），并拥有所有特色和人才来定位公司 欧洲水平的顶级球员。 我们很高兴成为一项创新和前瞻性项目的戳屋的合作伙伴，符合我们的投资策略，这是根据我们的投资策略，该战略基于宏观趋势中包括的公司的搜索，这将是经济，技术和社会演化的宏观趋势 未来几年。“
note_en:
Into this area has come European startup  Poke House, which is effectively industrializing the production of “poke bowls” for food delivery platforms. Poke House specializes in bowl food that often includes marinated fish that’s cubed and layered up with sticky rice, pickles, noodles, etc.
 The company has now raised €20 million ($24m) in a Series B funding round led by Eulero Capital, with the backing of FG2 Capital and reinvestment from Milan Investment Partners SGR. It using tech and data to optimize the production and delivery of its product via all the major food delivery platforms such as Uber East etc. The Italy-born food tech startup claims to have built a “€100M+ company” inside two years.
  Founded by Matteo Pichi and Vittoria Zanetti, Poke House has opened 30+ stores in Italy, Portugal and Spain, and now has 400 employees. It’s claiming an expected turnover of €40M+ in 2021.
 With the funding, the startup will start opening new stores in existing markets, enter France and start in expansion in the UK.
 Poke House says it uses a lot of tech on its back-end, tracking every element of the supply chain to optimize the business. It also analyzes data from third-party delivery platforms (ie. Deliveroo, Glovo, UberEats) to deliver a sub-10 mins food preparation time, and a delivery time under 25 mins.
 Matteo Pichi, Co-Founder of Poke House said: “The pandemic has challenged our food sector, and we see technology as the way forward to innovate and digitalize the traditional restaurant experience. We are seeing a shift in people’s desires in fast but healthy food. Poke bowls fit this new need and it promotes a more balanced, active and sustainable lifestyle with quick and healthy food options available nearby.”
 Speaking to TechCrunch, Pichi added: “Our competitors are the fast-growing healthy concepts such as Sweetgreen or Sweetfin in the US. But in the same time, we think we are lucky because we really are one of the first brands built 100% from food delivery experts or former employees. Our next competitors are gonna be full native virtual brands extremely strong in data analysis and digital brand building. We use food delivery platforms as media platforms and we invest heavier than competitors in the channel.”
 Gianfranco Burei, Founding Partner of Eulero Capital said: “Poke House business model rides some of the main trends in the food sector (food-tech, healthy food, delivery, customization) and has all the characteristics and talents to position the company among the top players at European level. We are thrilled to be a partner of Poke House in an innovative and forward-looking project, in line with our investment strategy which is based on the search for companies included in the macro-trends that will characterize the economic, technological and social evolution of the coming years.”
title: “碗食品”启动戳屋关闭由Eulero Capital领导的2400万系列B系列，在欧洲扩展
title_en: ‘Bowl food’ startup Poke House closes $24M Series B led by Eulero Capital to expand in Europe

Chennai和旧金山 - 总部费收费在周二上表示，它已在其Sapphire Ventures和现有投资者Tiger Global和Insight Venture Partners领导的融资系列系列筹集了1.25亿美元。
 新的融资融资价值10岁的起动14亿美元，自第六个月前系列自第六次以来为3倍。其他一些现有投资者还参加了新一轮，该费用筹集了2.3亿美元迄今为止。
  如果您是企业，设置和管理订阅服务 - 以确保重复收入 - 可能被证明是一个复杂的过程。您可能希望为新潜在客户提供免费为期30天的审判。如果有些客户想要移动到不同的定价层，怎么办？这些是收费的一些问题。
 费用将帮助个人，中小型企业和企业设置，管理和自动执行订阅，计费，发票和付款。
 费用的关键优势之一是它可以帮助大型企业在10天内转移到订阅模型。
 该行业正在经历一个“重大变革”，企业将自己的数字转变为搬迁到SaaS模型，Krish Subramanian，联合创始人和费用行政长官，在面试中告诉TechCrunch。这是这一变化，使收费是对数千家公司的收费。
 费用在印度东南海岸的一个城市的钦奈公寓。 Subramanian通过Goel Spolsky，Trello的创始人汇集了阅读博客帖子，作为启动自己的冒险的早期灵感。 
“他正在解决一个非常无聊的问题，但以非常有趣的方式，他曾经分享他在建立一家公司的故事，”他在早些时候的采访中说道。 “这是我的灵感，我应该像那样开始我的公司。因此，在在其他公司工作的同时，我们节省了足够的并获得了启动技能。“
  今天的启动产品不仅限于计费。它还有助于企业插头收入泄露，提高客户忠诚度，将新类别扩展到已准备好的新类别，并在30分钟内进行定价计划 - 引入和删除它们。
 它支持超过100种货币，以及数十间受欢迎的支付网关，包括条纹，Braintree，WorldPay和PayPal，其全球税收管理覆盖范围也有助于企业扩展到新市场。例如，MakeSpace，一家按需存储公司使用费用的服务，以便从四个市场缩放到31年，以一年。
 初创公司已经超过了2,500多名客户，其中大多数是基于美国和欧洲的。其中一些客户包括云软件OKTA，商业软件公司新鲜工作，日历邀请经理，培训平台Linux学院和日本科技巨头富士通等品牌。
 亚拉马裔人表示，费用伯的收入在过去12个月中翻了一番，客户的收入增长了125％，但他没有透露数字。
 他说，就像其他企业一样，费收费一直在谨慎导航全球大流行。他说，幸福六个月前确保了初创公司在银行有足够的资金来舒适地运营。
 但最近的增长费用已经看到促使创业公司变得更加激进。 “未来五年来，我们有一个机会窗口，让我们精美地建造这个类别并为很多客户提供服务，”他说。他说：这就是导致启动探索新融资的原因，并补充说，资本成本目前在市场上也发挥了作用。 
“随着订阅的全球转变普及的普遍性地发展，费用率为多个市场细分市场的新产品具有令人难以置信的大胆愿景，”Sapphire Ventures的合伙人在一份声明中举行的伙伴Rajeev Dham说。 “经过多年的认识，我对自己的思维和在建造费用中的努力作为正在重塑更广阔的空间的新兴的类别领导者最深刻的印象。”
 费用将部署新的资本，以扩大其产品套件，并在新功能上工作，以更好地帮助企业。
 周二的公告是在一片印度初创公司的时候筹集了大量资金，以更频繁的步伐，随着投资者在世界第二大互联网市场的有希望投注上的增加时，估值增加了估值。费用是本月第七个印度创业公司，而今年11次。
 印度初创公司社会商业Meesho，Fintech公司克服，电子药学公司药房，千禧一代的成长，商业消息平台Gupshup和社交网络Sharechat本月早些时候达到了独角兽状态。 TechCrunch上周举报了SoftBank正在谈判投资Zeta和Swiggy。周一的Razorpay宣布了新的筹款，价值为30亿美元。
note_en:
Chennai and San Francisco-headquartered  Chargebee said on Tuesday it has raised $125 million in its Series G financing round led by Sapphire Ventures and existing investors Tiger Global and Insight Venture Partners.
 The new financing round valued the 10-year-old startup at $1.4 billion, a  3x increase since the Series F round six months ago. Some other existing investors also participated in the new round, said Chargebee, which has raised $230 million to date.
  If you’re a business, setting up and managing a subscription service — to ensure recurring revenue — could prove to be a complex process. You may want to offer a free 30-day trial to new potential customers. What if some customers want to move to a different pricing tier? These are some of the problems Chargebee is equipped to handle.
 Chargebee helps individuals, small and medium-sized businesses and enterprises set up, manage, and automate subscriptions, billing, invoicing, and payments.
 One of the key strengths of Chargebee is that it can help even large enterprises move to a subscription model within 10 days.
 The industry is going through a “significant change” with businesses digitally transforming themselves and moving to the SaaS model, Krish Subramanian, co-founder and chief executive of Chargebee, told TechCrunch in an interview. And it’s this change that has made Chargebee so vital to thousands of companies today.
 Chargebee was founded in an apartment in Chennai, a city on India’s southeastern coast. Subramanian has credited reading blog posts by Joel Spolsky, founder of Trello, as an early inspiration to start his own venture.
 “He was solving a very boring problem but in very interesting ways, and he used to share the story of how he is building a company,” he  said in an earlier interview. “That was my inspiration that I should start my company like that. So while working at other companies we saved enough and acquired skills to start this.”
  The startup’s offerings today are not limited to just billing. It also helps businesses plug revenue leakage, increase customer loyalty, expand into new categories with the backend ready, and experiment with pricing plans — introducing and removing them within 30 minutes.
 It supports over 100 currencies, and dozens of popular payment gateways, including Stripe, Braintree, WorldPay and PayPal, and its global tax management coverage also helps businesses to expand to new markets. MakeSpace, an on-demand storage company, used Chargebee’s services to scale from four markets to 31 in one year, for instance.
 The startup has amassed over 2,500 customers, most of whom are based in the U.S. and Europe. Some of these customers include brands such as cloud software Okta, business software firm Freshworks, calendar invites manager Calendly, training platform Linux Academy, and Japanese tech giant Fujitsu.
 Subramanian said Chargebee’s revenue has doubled in the past 12 months and customer’s revenue has grown by 125%, though he didn’t disclose figures.
 He said that like other businesses, Chargebee has been cautiously navigating the global pandemic. The fundraise six months ago ensured that the startup had enough capital in the bank to operate comfortably, he said.
 But the recent growth Chargebee has seen prompted the startup to grow more aggressive. “There’s a window of opportunity for the next five years for us to build out this category beautifully and serve a lot of customers,” he said. “And that’s what led the startup to explore the new financing round, he said, adding that the fact that the cost of capital is lower currently in the market also played a role.
 “As the global shift to subscription-first models continue to grow in popularity, Chargebee has an incredibly bold vision for new products for multiple market segments,” said Rajeev Dham, Partner at Sapphire Ventures, in a statement. “After years of knowing them, I’ve been most impressed by their thoughtfulness and execution in building Chargebee as the emerging category leader that is reinventing the broader space.”
 Chargebee will deploy the fresh capital to expand its suite of products and work on new capabilities to help enterprises in even more ways.
 Tuesday’s announcement comes at a time when a slice of Indian startups are raising large amounts of capital at a much more frequent pace and at increased valuations as investors double down on promising bets in the world’s second-largest internet market. Chargebee is the seventh Indian startup to turn a unicorn this month, and 11th this year.
 Indian startups social commerce Meesho, fintech firm  CRED, e-pharmacy firm PharmEasy, millennials-focused  Groww,  business messaging platform Gupshup and  social network ShareChat attained the unicorn status earlier this month. TechCrunch reported last week that SoftBank is in talks to  invest in Zeta and  Swiggy. Razorpay on Monday  announced new fundraise that valued it at $3 billion.
title: 预计12500万美元的筹款14亿美元的费用
title_en: Chargebee valued at $1.4 billion in new $125 million fundraise

2017年，基于Goa的前记者Anoop Babani，当他在1931年遇到一本书时，在世界各地的骑自行车的人遇到了一本书，在普遍铺设道路和卫星通信前的三个印度人遇到了一本书。骑在世界各地。
 “新中国出生在世界上，”他们在他们的一个目的地写道。 “很少见她;但那些看的人，意识到她如何影响世界的课程和塑造世界的历史。“骑自行车的人 -  Adi Hakim，Jal Bapasola和Rustom Bhumgara-Chronicled的整个，不寻常，四年半的探险。在20世纪初印度，骑自行车是为了通勤。从未被视为一种看世界的方式。
 “他们不仅仅是骑自行车的人，”巴巴尼说，除了他的妻子，艺术家作家和狂热的骑自行车的萨维亚·韦加，开始研究探险。他们发现骑自行车者如何回来告诉印度更多关于一个不断变化的世界 - 并激励他人采取类似的旅程：四个这样的全球探险，跨越1923年至1942年，涉及六名骑自行车的人。所有在小规模快速的人都来自孟买的Parsi社区（然后孟买）。
 巴比尼和Viegas收集了档案信息，采访了家庭成员，并找到了照片。他们的工作导致了一张照片展览会，我们的马鞍，我们的屁股，他们的世界，现在采取了一本书的形式，自行车日记：印度骑自行车者和世界各地的令人难以置信的旅程。结果是在广大社会和政治变革中的战争之间的世界一瞥。它也将冒险骑自行车者降落了几次。
  Adi Hakim是Bombay举重俱乐部的成员，具有户外活动的诀窍。在自行车日记中，他的孙女Jasmine Marshall说，“没有什么是不可能的，”他总是告诉我。一个激情，我相信，由他所有的自行车伴侣分享。“其中有六个，包括Bapasola，Bhumgara和三个人开始大探险，但没有完成它。这并不是完全清楚这些历史上这些年轻人（一个辩护琐事主义的民族主义团体）以及追随他们的人，但巴巴的人认为这是他们参与印度自由斗争的方式。 “而不是挥舞着旗帜和加入示威活动，他们选择了在危险的道路上踏板，”他说，“为世界绘制了一个真正的印度图片，这些图片将描绘我们祖国的光荣文明，文化和建筑。”
 探险队于1923年10月15日离开孟买。当他们于1928年3月返回时，他们已经到了27个国家，并踏上了40,000多英里的旁遮普邦，俾路支省，中东，欧洲，美国，日本和东南亚洲。它们遇到了浓郁的森林，沼泽和阿尔卑斯山的可怕孤独。
 每个人都骑着皇家的Benson骑自行车，携带英国护照，一些衣服和医学，自行车修理工具，使用的指南针，世界地图和卢比。 2,000  - 约27美元。但他们也带来了技巧旅行。 Hakim是Chronicler，Bhumgara一辆训练有素的汽车机械师，该机械师曾经执行过马戏团的杂技，以及Bapasola地图阅读器。他们在印度骑自行车的前三个月，然后进入现在是伊朗，在哪里赚钱，不加勒比尔拉在牙齿上走了五次乘客。从那里他们去了巴格达，以及在叙利亚美岛沙漠中，他们在23天越过。家庭成员描述了这一点是他们旅程中最糟糕的一部分 - 以及一种现象的成就。 
Hovji Hakim，Hakim的七个儿子中的三分之一，在书中说，他父亲的一个人让他们都说他们的最后祈祷。 “幸运的是，一些贝都因人发现了他们，”他说。 “每次父亲对我都叙述这个故事时，他会突然沉默，谦卑他活着。”
 “我们的生活，我们被告知，从他们的掠夺性游览中造成严重危险，”他们写了一篇帮助他们穿过沙漠的人。 “相反，在整个旅程中，我们发现贝都斯比敌人更多的朋友。”
 然后海上航行从埃及的亚历山大到意大利布林迪西。在罗马，他们从那时起就签名，然后是总理·贝尼托·梅索洛尼，在梵蒂冈，他们有一个教皇Pius Xi的观众。欧洲当时仍然深深地嘲笑第一次世界大战：贫困，道路，不卫生的条件。在罗马，他们被误认为是德国间谍，并在酒吧后花了一个夜晚。但总理的签名和关于他们在罗马报纸之旅的新闻报道支持他们的差不多的故事。
  在筹集欧洲的一些钱后，他们用他们的自行车向纽约航行，他们发现“迷惑”。他们在摩天大楼，交通和轻巧者之间度过了两周。他们最初的骑行伴侣之一，Gustad Hathiram决定留在美国。 （他成为佛罗里达州的汽车机械师，并于1973年死亡。）
 剩下的骑自行车者在美国踩踏的未来五个月，在美国大学的数百名印第安人热烈地接受过。在底特律，他们短暂地为福特汽车公司工作，大概是标志性模型T的装配线。
 他们特别敬畏美国道路。 “这是我们旅途中的第一次，我们可以在五天内每天每天骑100英里，”他们从波士顿到布法罗的旅行写道。他们发现了美国经验的其他部分令人沮丧，如粗鲁和侮辱移民局。他们写道，“该移民们处于最宽容，并被怀疑地观察。”它们同样受到“在铁路列车，火车站，在电车车站，酒店，在娱乐场所在铁路列车中观察到的最令人沮丧的角色的种族歧视。”他们继续，“印度对他们来说是一个半野蛮的国家，被黑色数字，神秘主义者，魔术师和蛇和老虎感染。”
  从旧金山从1925年11月航行到日本。从那里他们去了大陆，成为冬季韩国“赫尔米特王国”的第一个循环。这将它们带入中国，脉动着暴力的政治变革。当他们怀疑被怀疑是间谍时，步枪被指出。在印度主义（当天越南），他们对移民规则感到愤怒，以及印度人如何被法国殖民政府对待。 Bapasola为当地报纸写了一篇关于它的文章，此时他们被捕 - 再次被捕 - 就“引入仇恨和蔑视（法国）的印度政府”的指控！“他们写。他们只在Siagon的州长莫里斯·安托尼·弗朗索斯Monguillot才能在Siagon的州长莫里斯·弗朗索斯Monguillot之后释放。 
最后，1928年3月，他们返回孟买和一个激动的招待会。沿着他们获得热烈欢迎的方式，被授予26枚奖牌，发表公共讲座，并收集了146人签名：国王，教皇，总理，总统，工业家，市长 - 即使是满洲军护腕。
 他们的努力激发了他人。 1924年1月，体育记者弗拉梅尔达沃瓦继续进行独奏探险。他在维也纳，古斯塔夫Sztavjanik挑选了一个旅行合作伙伴，并在52个国家循环七年骑在一起。 “他们是最艰难，最冒险的旅程，”巴纳尼说：西非，撒哈拉，亚马逊，安第斯山脉。
  1933年4月，来自孟买kaikeeKharas，Rustam Ghandhi的另一个三重奏，并开始了九年的九千英里。 “他们是第一个在铁窗帘阿富汗骑自行车的骑车人，”巴纳尼说。他们出席了Adolf Hitler的历史纽伦堡纳齐纳粹派对，并是第一个跨越澳大利亚，新西兰和印度尼西亚的赛车。跟随两个独奏探险。 1934年5月，十八岁的Jamshed Mody左，三年来达到3万英里。 Manek Vajifdar在同月离开了，但由于国际冲突，四年后结束了他在伦敦的旅程。总而言之，探险队导致了六十多本书，向印度人开辟了世界，谁可能从未有机会看到它。
 在回归后，原来的三名骑自行车者被选为，但印度英国政府没有正式认可，这可能是他们努力更多的原因。 “当时骑自行车旅游是一个完全不同的球比赛。这是一个上层阶级的追求，由美国和欧洲骑自行车的人在20世纪初发起和引入，“巴纳尼说。 “少数印度骑自行车者被它留下了深刻的印象，并带着它。因此，当时统治英国帝国承认他们的行为是必不可少的。由于这没有发生，骑自行车的人和他们的旅程直到最近完全忘记了。“
  在一些荣耀中晒太阳后，所有三个骑自行车者都归还定期生活。哈基姆在石油工业中养老了。保姆拉加入了独立运动，协助印度国会，并在汽车行业工作。 Bapasola加入了一家钢铁公司。
 Babani和Viegas现在正在推动博物馆和公共纪念碑，以尊重所有骑自行车的人。巴巴尼说，他看到了对印度休闲骑自行车的复苏兴趣：销售优质自行车，局部骑自行车俱乐部的爆炸，新的耐力骑自行车活动。但骑自行车？这仍然是一个终极挑战。 “他们的成就和冒险精神在这个国家留下了一个不可磨灭的印记，”巴巴尼说，“以及灵感来源。”
note_en:
In 2017, Anoop Babani, Goa-based former journalist, was recuperating from a cycling accident when he encountered a book from 1931,  With Cyclists Around the World, written by three Indians who, in the days before widespread paved roads and satellite communications, had biked around the world.
 “A new China has been born in the world,” they wrote prophetically of one of their destinations. “Few see her; but those who do see, realize how she will affect world’s course and shape world’s history.” The cyclists—Adi Hakim, Jal Bapasola, and Rustom Bhumgara—chronicled their entire, unusual, four-and-a-half-year expedition. In early 20th-century India, cycling was for commuting. It was never considered a way to see the world.
 “They were much more than mere cyclists,” says Babani who, along with his wife, artist-writer and fellow avid cyclist Savia Viegas, began to research the expedition in greater depth. They found how the cyclists returned to tell India more about a changing world—and inspire others to take similar journeys: four more such global expeditions, spanning 1923 to 1942, involving six more cyclists. All who partook in the small-scale craze were from the Parsi community of Mumbai (then Bombay).
 Babani and Viegas collected archival information, interviewed family members, and found photographs. Their work resulted in a photo exhibition,  Our Saddles, Our Butts, Their World, and has now taken the form of a book,   The Bicycle Diaries: Indian Cyclists and Their Incredible Journeys Around the World. The result is a glimpse of a world between wars, in the midst of vast social and political change. It also landed the adventuring cyclists in jail a few times.
  Adi Hakim was a member of the Bombay Weightlifting Club with a knack for outdoor activities. In  The Bicycle Diaries, his granddaughter Jasmine Marshall says, “‘Nothing is impossible,’ he would always tell me. A passion, I am sure, shared by all his bicycle mates.” There were six of those, including Bapasola, Bhumgara, and three others who started the grand expedition but did not complete it. It’s not completely clear what inspired these young Parsis (an ethnoreligious group that practices Zoroastrianism) and the ones who followed them, but Babani believes it was their way of participating in India’s freedom struggle. “Instead of waving flags and joining demonstrations, they chose to pedal on a perilous path,” he says, “to paint for the world a true picture of India that would depict the glorious civilization, culture, and architecture of our native land.”
 The expedition departed Mumbai in October 15, 1923. By the time they returned in March 1928, they had been to 27 countries and pedaled more than 40,000 miles—through Punjab, Balochistan, the Middle East, Europe, United States, Japan, and Southeast Asia. They encountered dense forests, swamps, and the terrible solitude of the Alps.
 Each man rode a Royal Benson bicycle and carried just a British passport, some clothes and medicine, bicycle repair tools, a used compass, a world map, and Rs. 2,000—about $27. But they also brought skills to the trip. Hakim was the chronicler, Bhumgara a trained automobile mechanic known to perform circus-like acrobatics, and Bapasola the map reader. They spent the first three months cycling through India, and then into what is now Iran, where, to earn money, Bhumgara pulled a car with five passengers down the road with his teeth. From there they went to Baghdad, and across the Syrian-Mesopotamian desert, which they crossed in 23 days. Family members describe this as the worst part of their journey—and a phenomenal achievement.
  Lovji Hakim, the third of Hakim’s seven sons, says in the book that at one point his father had them all say their last prayers. “Fortunately, some Bedouins found them,” he says. “Every time my father narrated this story to me, he would suddenly go silent and humbled that he was alive.”
 “Our lives, we were told, were in grave danger from their predatory excursions,” they wrote of the people who helped them get through the desert. “Instead, throughout our journey we found the Bedouins more a friend than an enemy.”
 A sea voyage then took them from Alexandria in Egypt to Brindisi, Italy. In Rome, they got an autograph from then–Prime Minister Benito Mussolini, and at the Vatican they had an audience with Pope Pius XI. Europe at the time was still deeply scarred by World War I: poverty, bad roads, and unhygienic conditions. In Rome they were mistaken for German spies and spent a night behind bars. But the Prime Minister’s autograph and a news report about their journey in a Roman newspaper backed their otherwise far-fetched story.
  After raising some money in Europe, they sailed with their bicycles to New York, which they found “bewitching.” They spent two weeks among the skyscrapers, traffic, and flappers. One of their initial riding companions, Gustad Hathiram, decided to stay in the United States. (He became an auto mechanic in Florida, and died in 1973.)
 The remaining cyclists spent the next five months pedaling across the United States, where they were warmly received by hundreds of Indians studying in American universities. In Detroit, they briefly worked for the Ford Motor Company, presumably on the assembly line for the iconic Model T.
 They were particularly in awe of American roads. “It was for the first time in our journey that we could ride 100 miles a day consecutively for five days,” they wrote of the trip from Boston to Buffalo. They found other parts of the American experience disheartening, such as the rude and insulting immigration authorities. “The immigrant is at best tolerated and viewed with suspicion,” they wrote. They were equally appalled by the “racial discrimination of the most demeaning character which is observed in railway trains, at railway stations, in tramcars, in hotels, and in places of amusements.” They continued, “India to them is a semi-barbarous country inhabited by black figures, mystics, magicians and infested with snakes and tigers.”
  From San Francisco they sailed to Japan in November 1925. From there they went to the mainland and became the first to cycle across the “Hermit Kingdom” of Korea in winter. This brought them into China, pulsing with violent political change. Rifles were pointed at their heads when they were suspected of being spies. In Indochina (present-day Vietnam), they were furious about immigration rules and how Indians were treated by the French colonial government. Bapasola wrote an article about it for a local newspaper, at which point they were arrested—again—on charges of “bringing into hatred and the contempt of (French) government of Indo-China!” they wrote. They were only released after the intervention by Siagon’s Governor, Maurice Antoine Francois Monguillot, a sportsman himself.
 Finally, in March 1928, they returned to Mumbai and a rousing reception. Along the way they had received warm welcomes, been awarded 26 medals, delivered public lectures, and collected 146 autographs: kings, the Pope, prime ministers, presidents, industrialists, mayors—even Zhang Zuolin, the Warlord of Manchuria.
 Their efforts inspired others. In January 1924, sports journalist Framroze Davar went on a solo expedition. He picked up a traveling partner in Vienna, Gustav Sztavjanik, and the two cycled together for seven years, across 52 countries. “Theirs was the toughest and most adventurous journey,” says Babani: West Africa, the Sahara, the Amazon, the Andes.
  In April 1933, another trio from Mumbai—Kaikee Kharas, Rustam Ghandhi, and Rutton Shroff—began a nine-year expedition of more than 50,000 miles. “They were the first cyclists ever to cycle across iron-curtained Afghanistan,” says Babani. They were present at the historic Nuremberg Nazi Party rally addressed by Adolf Hitler, and were the first to cycle across Australia, New Zealand, and Indonesia. Two more solo expeditions followed. Eighteen-year-old Jamshed Mody left in May 1934, and went 30,000 miles in three years. Manek Vajifdar departed the same month, but ended his journey in London four years later due to international conflicts. All told, the expeditions resulted in a half-dozen more books, and opened up the world to Indians back home who might never have had a chance to see it.
 Upon their return, the original three cyclists were lauded, but not formally recognized by the British government of India, which may be one reason their efforts have not been celebrated more. “At that time cycling tourism was a completely different ball game. It was an upper-class pursuit initiated and introduced predominantly by American and European cyclists in early 20th century,” says Babani. “A handful of Indian cyclists were impressed by it and took to it. Hence, it was imperative for the then-ruling British Empire to recognize their deeds. Since this did not happen, the cyclists and their journeys were totally forgotten until recently.”
  After basking in some glory, all three cyclists returned to regular life. Hakim had a long career in the oil industry. Bhumgara joined the independence movement, assisted the Indian National Congress, and worked in the auto industry. Bapasola joined a steel company.
 Babani and Viegas are now pushing for a museum and public monuments to honor all the cyclists. And Babani says that he’s seen a resurgent interest in recreational cycling in India: megastores selling premium bikes, an explosion in local cycling clubs, new endurance cycling events. But cycling around the world? That remains an ultimate challenge. “Their achievements and adventurous spirit have left an indelible imprint on the country,” Babani says, “as well as a source of inspiration.”
title: 印度为世界各地骑自行车的迷你狂热
title_en: India’s Mini-Craze for Bicycling Around the World

这是巴塞罗那周六的午餐时间，乔塞拉科科乔在市中心的意大利餐厅外等待。他的大型大量的黄色glovo背包在于他旁边，但他即将递送的顺序来自Ubereats。在过去的几个月里，马拉库科一直在为两种食物交付的应用程序工作，因为他从几个小时内获得的钱，他被允许为Glovo工作不足以生活。像马拉科科这样的送货车手一直在克服更好的规定来保护其劳动权利。现在监管终于来了，即使它将带来的变化是不确定性的。
 在未来几个月内，西班牙设立了批准开拓新法律，阐明西班牙估计的8,000名送货车的劳动权利。在什么可以为其他欧洲国家设定先例，法律将宣布，演出的经济骑手是工资劳动者，并强制交付应用公司开辟他们的算法黑匣子，向他们的骑手解释他们正在使用哪些变量来确定它们的变量来确定它们的变量应用分数。为西班牙而战的工会争取过度劳累，送货驾驶者差价不良，认为这是一个胜利。但有些人担心法律可能对那种像JoséMaracucho这样的许多车手产生意外后果，没有工作许可。
  当Maracucho于2020年2月抵达委内瑞拉的巴塞罗那时，他听说找到工作的最快方式是为西班牙送货启动Glovo工作的骑手。他没有必要的论文来工作，但他找到了一位西班牙本地人，他租了他的加沃福账户来工作。他有一辆自行车，开始立即交付。 “我开始与Glovo一起使用，因为在没有文件的情况下到达时，你可以获得最简单的事情，”马拉科乔说。通过租用的账户，马拉科科可以在他所希望的时候工作，但他不得不向他租用它的人支付30％的盈利。账户的所有者还将其租到了四个人，以便此特定的Glovo账户不断提供订单。
 去年12月，Maracucho设法正规化了他的情况，并获得了自己的账户，但他的收入崩溃了。这是因为传动会，除了在大流行期间降低利率，不允许他每天工作超过2小时。该应用程序使用基于点的系统进行操作，以便当新的骑手获取许可证上，只能让他们超过100分的分数90分。为了获得更多工作时间的权利，马拉科诺需要增加他的分数 - 根据骑士的估计，您可以在每天四到八小时工作95分。 “你必须很难增加你的积分并获得更多时间，这就是为什么我合并在Glovo工作，在Ubereats工作，＆＃34;马拉库科说。
  Glovo的算法建立了骑手的分数。为了确定得分 - 并增加它 - 公司依赖于随时运送速度，客户评级或可用性等因素，以及不与骑手共享的其他因素。这是西班牙的新立法旨在瞄准这个问题。推动Gig经济的算法 - 从Glovo和Stuart到Ubereats和Surferoo  - 可以随时调整，对控制它们的公司更有利。
 “他们告诉你作为自由职业者很酷，因为你可以决定什么时候工作。但最后，你意识到你必须在他们问你时工作。在Glovo，如果你不接受订单，你就会失去[积分]“，他表示，创造了西班牙骑手集体骑手X Derechos的NúriaSot。除了律师集体ColiCerence ColiCerectiu Ronda，Riders X Derechos在巴塞罗那策划了几个法院案件，争论演出经济公司依赖于侵犯工人基本权利的剥削和不公平的惩罚制度 - 例如，惩罚罢工，或者不接受订单。 “我才得到两次得分，我不知道为什么，”马拉科科解释。 “人们对工人的影响不了解这一点，但是当我得分严重得分时，我丢失了积分，因此工作时间。”
  基础上这些公司的算法帮助他们的利润飙升 - 加沃在2015年的巴塞尔诺曼出生，现在在20个国家提供，其中一些估计将其估值达到1.2亿英镑。但是，来自骑士X Derechos，工会和律师等组织的压力导致西班牙政府规范演出经济算法，并重新思考公司和工人之间的关系。 
经过近五个月的谈判，在2021年3月，西班牙政府与议会批准的议会批准，西班牙政府达成协议。法令确定了工人不是自雇但工资劳动者的推定。它还迫使这些公司通知工人的法律代表，了解确定决定的算法的内部运作，这些算法“可能影响工作条件，并获取和维护就业，包括分析”。
 当法律批准时，西班牙决定强迫数字公司向其工人开放部分算法可能会对欧盟产生影响，这已经与雇主谈判达成了类似的协议。
  “开放算法超出了保障民主和劳工权利的全部元素，”卡塔诺伊秘书秘书Carlos del Barrio，Catalonia部门部门政策和可持续性，在Obreras（CC.oo）的领土政策和社会行动，两个最大的工会之一在国内。 Del Barrio认为，法律将开始抓住骑手的骑手权利，潜水剥削。 “算法在性别和性别的基础上歧视许多其他因素，”他声称。
  Gig经济公司并无欢迎协议。在通过新的法规后，APS，包括Glovo，Uber Eats，DelignoO和Stuart的贸易团体声称必须披露他们的算法“毫无疑问地对西班牙数字经济的发展非常负面影响，以及侵权企业自由和工业产权自由的最基本原则。 Glovo也不差价响应评论请求。优步发言人表示，该公司“完全致力于提高工作标准，并在保持灵活性和控制的同时更具益处”。
 巴塞罗那超级计算中心的人工智能科学协调员ulisescortés表示，互联网的指数增长部分受到缺乏强劲立法调节其使用的推动。 “没有人想到立法使用私人数据;因此，到目前为止，没有对数字平台的法律规定。“虽然珍贵算法开放可以使公司失去竞争优势，但Cortés说，这条规则最终将为一个经常挥舞其算法的行业带来一些公平，因为他们永远不会受到监管的推定。
 但即使这可能还不足以在演出经济模型的核心处固定腐烂，这仍然依然堆叠在骑手上。 Maracucho说，像许多需要赚钱的其他移民车手一样，有兴趣能够在他能够尽可能多的时间工作。他担心，如果法律获得批准，格洛沃将雇用他们的小时数和较低的工资。与此同时，Maracucho认识到当前条件不好：公司支付很少 - 根据CC.OO，Glovo目前每令每次订单支付1.60欧元（1.39英镑）加上里程 - 降低费用，没有咨询，不包括员工假期。他认为得分制度是不公平的，应该受到监管。
 联合国和律师指出，该法令不会强迫公司雇用工人 - 它将只是假设他们应该这样做。但即使是理所当然的公司将雇用工人，新法律将使数千名不规则工人从系统中留出。这些是促进了平台增长的工人，这是由无情的钢管和偶尔悬停在演出经济的边缘的恐怖主义者。根据Carlos del Barrio的说法，租赁账户 - 正如Maracucho过去所做的那样 - 在西班牙非常普遍。仅在加泰罗尼亚，每次许可都有大约五名车手。 “有些人以遭受最绝对的强制性的牺牲品为代价赚钱，只要没有人想要采取的订单，没有人想工作的时间”，德尔巴罗奥尔说。 
马拉科科很清楚：“我怀疑西班牙人将拿起自行车并开始交付。他说，平台归功于不规则工作的人，“他说。 “他们现在与没有论文的所有骑手一起做了什么，他们基本上持续了平台并提出了它们？”
